items.answer_rationale: Black delivers a decisive mating attack starting with Rg2+, which gives check to the White king on h2. White has only two options: if Kh1, then Qh5# is immediate checkmate (the queen on h5 is supported and delivers mate to the trapped king on h1). If instead Kh3, Black has overwhelming threats including Qh5 with unstoppable mate, as the king has no escape squares. The rook on g2 cuts off retreat squares while the queen delivers the final blow. This is a forced checkmate sequence.

items.answer_rationale: Ng5 is the winning move, attacking both e6 and h6. Black's best defense is Bf6 or moving the bishop away, but after Nxh6, White has a decisive attack on the weakened h-file. The h6 pawn removal exposes the black king to mating threats. For example, after the likely Bf6 (or another bishop move) Nxh6, White threatens Qh5 with mate following, forcing Black into a lost endgame. This forcing sequence wins material and creates an unstoppable attack.

After a2, White cannot prevent queening: if Ra3 (blocking), Black's rook and king coordinate to support promotion while White's knight cannot help defend. This forcing move guarantees victory as any attempt by White to stop the pawn fails to the coordination of Black's pieces and material advantage.

items.answer_rationale: Black plays Rf1+ forcing perpetual check. After Ke5, Black continues with Rf5+. The white king cannot escape the checks while the g7 pawn is still on the board - the rook controls key squares and maintains checks by moving along the f and 5th ranks. After White plays g8=Q, Black's rook continues to give perpetual checks (Rf8, etc.), preventing White from using the new queen or advancing the d-pawn. White cannot make progress and Black forces a draw by perpetual check.

items.answer_rationale: After Qxf7+ the Black king is forced to h8 (the only legal move). Then Qxh7# is checkmate—the queen on h7 checks the king on h8, which has no escape squares and cannot capture the queen or interpose. This is a forced mate in 2.

items.answer_rationale: Black plays Rxg2+ forcing Kxg2 (only legal move). Then Qc1 attacks the vulnerable rook on d1. The rook must move (Rd2 is forced). Black then plays Bxd4, winning the bishop since if Qxd4, Black recaptures with Qxd1, winning the exchange and maintaining a dominant position with a checkmating attack threat against the exposed white king.

items.answer_rationale: Qxf6+ is a forcing check that wins Black's undefended bishop. The Black king on g7 is in check from the queen on f6 and must move (Kh7, Kf8, Kg8, Kh6, or Kf7 are the only options). White gains a full piece with no compensation for Black. This is a simple tactical win that ends the game decisively.
